\documentclass{article}
% \added and \deleted come from the changes package, not inline \newcommand
% definitions — this matters because the regex-based config also matches
% command names inside \newcommand{\added}{...} bodies, which would mangle
% the definitions. Using a real package keeps both the original AND the
% cleaned source compilable.
\usepackage{changes}
\usepackage{xcolor}

\title{Revision-markup demo}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section{Introduction}
This paragraph contains a normal sentence
\added{plus a sentence reviewers asked us to add}
and \deleted{a sentence reviewers asked us to remove (this whole clause
should disappear after the converter runs).} The surrounding prose stays
intact.

\textcolor{red}{Red-highlighted phrasing} also gets unwrapped by the
config — only the inner text survives.

The brace-balanced matcher correctly handles
\added{nested commands like \emph{this italic} and \textbf{this bold}},
which a naive regex would mangle.

\section{Discussion}
% Comments like this one are stripped by the main pipeline, regardless of
% whether the user supplies a config.
The pipeline removes the markup wrappers but preserves the surviving
prose, so the arXiv version reads as a clean paper without a noisy diff.

\end{document}
